text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction}} is defined as: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction
Description: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction' es: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Multiple 'R'}} is defined as: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: The Multiple 'R'
Description: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'The Multiple 'R'' es: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Strong R}} is defined as: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Strong R
Description: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Strong R' es: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::L-Vocalization}} is defined as: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: L-Vocalization
Description: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'L-Vocalization' es: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatalization of T/D}} is defined as: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Palatalization of T/D
Description: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Palatalization of T/D' es: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).

Rule: S-Voicing & Palatalization
Description: Syllable-final 's' or 'z' adjusts to its environment:

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::EP Vowel Omission (Elision)}} is defined as: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: EP Vowel Omission (Elision)
Description: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'EP Vowel Omission (Elision)' es: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).

usage:

Description: In Brazilian Portuguese, common words contract heavily in casual speech:

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::BP L-Vocalization}} is defined as: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: BP L-Vocalization
Description: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'BP L-Vocalization' es: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::BP Plural Marker Dropping}} is defined as: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: BP Plural Marker Dropping
Description: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'BP Plural Marker Dropping' es: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Stress
Description: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Stress' es: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::European Portuguese}} is defined as: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: European Portuguese
Description: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'European Portuguese' es: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Brazilian Portuguese}} is defined as: More syllable-timed and musical.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Brazilian Portuguese
Description: More syllable-timed and musical.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Brazilian Portuguese' es: More syllable-timed and musical.

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction}}? In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction
In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction'? In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts}}? To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts
To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts'? To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: Tip
The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'Tip'? The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.

text: In Portuguese, the tilde accent (~) indicates {{c1::nasalization}} of the vowel. The common ending '-ão' is pronounced as a nasal diphthong {{c2::/ɐ̃w̃/}}.
explanation: The air goes through both the mouth and the nose when pronouncing these vowels. Essential for correct Portuguese accent.
usage: Examples: pão (/pɐ̃w̃/ - bread), não (/nɐ̃w̃/ - no), coração (heart).
spanish: En portugués, el acento de virgulilla (~) indica nasalización. La terminación '-ão' suena como el diptongo nasal '/ɐ̃w̃/'.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Bom dia, tia."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Bom djia, tchia.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Palatalization of T/D (BR)
In Brazilian Portuguese, /d/ and /t/ are palatalized into affricates [dʒ] and [tʃ] when preceding the vowel /i/.
usage: Formal: Bom dia, tia. → Connected: Bom djia, tchia.
spanish: Spanish: Buenos días, tía.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "O Brasil ganhou sob o sol."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::O Brasiw ganhou sob o sow.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: L-Vocalization (BR)
Syllable-final L is vocalized to the semivowel [w] in Brazilian dialects.
usage: Formal: O Brasil ganhou sob o sol. → Connected: O Brasiw ganhou sob o sow.
spanish: Spanish: Brasil ganó bajo el sol.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Isto é excelente."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Isto é shs-lẽt.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Vowel Reduction (PT)
In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are almost completely deleted, leaving consonant clusters.
usage: Formal: Isto é excelente. → Connected: Isto é shs-lẽt.
spanish: Spanish: Esto es excelente.
